name: state
context: Terraform state tracks the mapping between your configuration and real-world infrastructure resources
info: Terraform state is a JSON file (terraform.tfstate) that records the binding between every resource block in your configuration and the real infrastructure object it manages. State stores resource metadata, dependency relationships, and cached attribute values so that Terraform can determine what changes need to be applied. Without state, Terraform has no way to know which cloud resources it controls. State is the source of truth for Terraform — it is consulted before every plan and apply to compute the diff between desired and actual infrastructure. For teams, state must be stored remotely (see [[terraform/state/backends]]) with locking (see [[terraform/state/locking]]) to prevent concurrent corruption. Existing infrastructure can be brought under management via [[terraform/state/import]].
body:
# Terraform State

Terraform state is the backbone of how Terraform maps your HCL configuration to real infrastructure. Every time you run `terraform apply`, Terraform writes a state file that records which real-world object corresponds to each resource block. This state file — `terraform.tfstate` — is a JSON document that Terraform consults on every subsequent operation.

## What State Tracks

State records three categories of information for every managed resource:

1. **Resource metadata** — the resource type, name, provider, and unique ID of the real-world object
2. **Attribute cache** — the last-known attribute values (IP addresses, ARNs, IDs) so Terraform can compute diffs without querying every API
3. **Dependency graph** — explicit and implicit dependencies between resources, used to determine creation and destruction order

```json
{
  "version": 4,
  "terraform_version": "1.7.0",
  "serial": 42,
  "lineage": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
  "outputs": {
    "instance_ip": {
      "value": "54.123.45.67",
      "type": "string"
    }
  },
  "resources": [
    {
      "mode": "managed",
      "type": "aws_instance",
      "name": "web",
      "provider": "provider[\"registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws\"]",
      "instances": [
        {
          "schema_version": 1,
          "attributes": {
            "id": "i-0abcdef1234567890",
            "ami": "ami-0c55b159cbfafe1f0",
            "instance_type": "t3.micro",
            "public_ip": "54.123.45.67"
          }
        }
      ]
    }
  ]
}
```

The `serial` increments with every state change. The `lineage` is a UUID assigned at `terraform init` that prevents accidentally applying state from a different project.

## State as Source of Truth

Terraform treats state as the authoritative record of what it manages. During `terraform plan`:

1. Terraform reads the current state file
2. Terraform refreshes state by querying each provider API for actual resource attributes
3. Terraform compares refreshed state against your configuration
4. The diff becomes the execution plan

If a resource exists in state but not in your config, Terraform plans to destroy it. If a resource exists in your config but not in state, Terraform plans to create it. This is why accidentally deleting or corrupting your state file is dangerous — Terraform will attempt to recreate everything.

## State CLI Commands

Terraform provides the `terraform state` subcommand for direct state manipulation:

```bash
# List all resources tracked in state
terraform state list

# Show detailed attributes for a specific resource
terraform state show aws_instance.web

# Move a resource to a new address (refactoring)
terraform state mv aws_instance.web aws_instance.app_server

# Remove a resource from state without destroying it
terraform state rm aws_instance.legacy

# Pull remote state to stdout (useful for inspection)
terraform state pull

# Push a local state file to the remote backend
terraform state push

# Replace a provider in state (provider migration)
terraform state replace-provider hashicorp/aws registry.terraform.io/hashicorp/aws
```

`terraform state mv` is essential when refactoring — renaming a resource block without `mv` would cause Terraform to destroy the old resource and create a new one.

`terraform state rm` removes Terraform's knowledge of a resource without touching the real infrastructure. Use this when you want to stop managing a resource without destroying it.

## State and Collaboration

The default local state file (`terraform.tfstate` on disk) breaks down in teams:

- **No shared access** — teammates cannot see or update the same state
- **No locking** — two people running `terraform apply` simultaneously can corrupt state
- **No versioning** — accidental deletion or corruption has no recovery path

Remote backends solve all three problems. See [[terraform/state/backends]] for configuring S3, GCS, Azure Blob, Consul, or Terraform Cloud as your state store, and [[terraform/state/locking]] for preventing concurrent modifications.

## Sensitive Data in State

State files contain **every attribute value** for every managed resource, including:

- Database passwords passed via `password` arguments
- Private keys generated by `tls_private_key`
- Secret values from `random_password`
- API tokens stored in resource attributes

State is stored as **plain-text JSON**. Critical security measures:

- **Encrypt at rest** — use backend encryption (S3 SSE, GCS encryption, Azure Storage encryption)
- **Encrypt in transit** — backends should enforce TLS
- **Restrict access** — limit state file access to authorized CI/CD pipelines and operators
- **Never commit state to git** — add `*.tfstate` and `*.tfstate.backup` to `.gitignore`
- **Use `sensitive = true`** — mark outputs as sensitive to prevent them from appearing in CLI output (but they are still in state)

```hcl
# .gitignore for Terraform projects
*.tfstate
*.tfstate.*
.terraform/
.terraform.lock.hcl
```

sections.title: What state tracks
sections.body: State records resource metadata (type, name, provider, real-world ID), cached attribute values (IPs, ARNs, computed fields), and the dependency graph between resources. The state file uses JSON format version 4 with a `serial` number that increments on every write and a `lineage` UUID that identifies the state's origin project. The `resources` array contains entries for each managed resource with full attribute snapshots.

sections.title: State as source of truth
sections.body: Terraform treats state as the canonical record of managed infrastructure. During `terraform plan`, it refreshes state by querying provider APIs, then diffs refreshed state against configuration. Resources in state but not config are destroyed; resources in config but not state are created. Corrupted or deleted state causes Terraform to lose track of all managed resources, potentially leading to duplicate resource creation.

sections.title: State CLI commands
sections.body: `terraform state list` shows all tracked resources. `terraform state show <address>` displays attributes for one resource. `terraform state mv <src> <dst>` renames a resource in state to match refactored config. `terraform state rm <address>` stops managing a resource without destroying it. `terraform state pull` and `push` transfer state between local and remote. `terraform state replace-provider` updates provider references during migrations.

sections.title: State and team collaboration
sections.body: Local state files fail in teams because there is no shared access, no locking, and no versioning. Remote backends (see [[terraform/state/backends]]) store state in a shared location with encryption and access controls. State locking (see [[terraform/state/locking]]) prevents two operators from running concurrent apply operations. Terraform Cloud and Enterprise add state versioning with rollback capabilities.

sections.title: Sensitive data in state
sections.body: State files contain plaintext values for all resource attributes, including passwords, private keys, and API tokens. Encrypt state at rest using backend encryption (S3 SSE, GCS encryption). Restrict access to state files via IAM policies or backend access controls. Never commit `*.tfstate` files to version control. Mark outputs as `sensitive = true` to suppress CLI display, but note that values are still stored in state as plaintext.

links.context: Configure remote storage for state files
S3 is a common backend for Terraform state storage
IAM policies control access to state files stored in AWS

related.note: Backends define where and how state is stored
Import brings existing resources into state management
CloudFormation is AWS's native IaC tool with its own state management via stacks

scripts.name: Terraform State Manager
scripts.description: Run terraform state operations: list, show, mv, rm, pull, push, import, replace-provider

TF_STATE_ACTION.description: State operation: list, show, mv, rm, pull, push, import, replace-provider

TF_STATE_RESOURCE.description: Resource address (for show, mv, rm, import) or state file path (for push)

TF_STATE_DESTINATION.description: Destination address (for mv) or new provider (for replace-provider)

TF_IMPORT_ID.description: Real-world resource ID for import operations

destination.description: Destination address for mv or new provider for replace-provider

resources.description: List of resource addresses (for list operation)

depends.reason: Working directory must be initialized to access state

frontmatter.author: dojo-community
frontmatter.audience: devops engineers, platform engineers, AI agents
